skill.name: backtesting-frameworks
skill.description: Build robust backtesting systems for trading strategies with guidance on look-ahead bias, survivorship bias, walk-forward analysis, transaction costs, and performance metrics.
skill.summary: Design and evaluate trading strategy backtests with realistic assumptions and risk controls.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported code execution, command execution, network, credential, weak crypto, reconnaissance, and combined-risk patterns. Review found these are Markdown examples, trading terminology, Python type hints, dictionary key handling, and ordinary resource links, with no evidence of malicious intent or prompt injection.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fences Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The backtick findings occur in Markdown fences that delimit diagrams and Python examples. They do not instruct the assistant to execute shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are Markdown code-block delimiters and section boundaries. No shell command, command substitution, or execution instruction is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Function Type Hint Flagged as Dynamic Execution
low_findings.description: The reported dynamic function constructor is explanatory text for a strategy callback signature. It is not JavaScript code and does not create or execute code dynamically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line appears inside a Python docstring and describes a callable interface. There is no eval, Function constructor, or generated executable string.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Finance Terms Flagged as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The weak crypto detections map to trading and metrics language such as bias, Sharpe ratio, drawdown, and related strategy terms. No cryptographic API is used.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected context is financial backtesting terminology and metric calculation. I found no use of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or other weak cryptographic primitives.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Dictionary Keys Flagged as Credential Files
low_findings.description: The sensitive-file detection occurs where example Python code reads parameter names from a dictionary. It does not reference certificates, private keys, or secret files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line handles Python dictionary keys in a grid-search example. There is no filesystem path, credential material, or certificate handling.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Resource Links Flagged as Network Behavior
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are bibliography and documentation links. They do not fetch data, send user information, or direct the assistant to make network requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are static Markdown links to books and documentation. I found no code path that transmits data to those locations.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Reconnaissance and Combined-Risk Heuristic
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance and critical combination findings are not supported by the reviewed context. The file contains instructional backtesting material and examples only.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines discuss robust systems, signal shifting, parameter search, and point-in-time data. I found no system probing, credential access, or exfiltration flow.

content.value_statement: Trading strategy tests can mislead when they ignore bias, costs, or out-of-sample validation. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through practical backtesting structures and checks.
actual_capabilities: Explains event-driven and vectorized backtesting designs for trading strategies.
Shows how to account for commissions, slippage, position state, and fills.
Guides walk-forward optimization with training and testing windows.
Provides Monte Carlo methods for drawdown and loss probability analysis.
Defines performance metrics including Sharpe, Sortino, Calmar, win rate, and profit factor.
Highlights common sources of backtest bias and practical mitigations.
limitations: Does not include packaged source files beyond the instructional Markdown skill.
Does not provide market data, broker integration, or live trading execution.
Example code requires adaptation before production use.
Financial results still depend on data quality and user assumptions.

use_cases.title: Validate a New Strategy
use_cases.description: Structure a backtest that separates training, validation, and test periods with realistic trading costs.

use_cases.title: Design Backtesting Infrastructure
use_cases.description: Compare event-driven and vectorized designs before building an internal strategy research platform.

use_cases.title: Review Performance Claims
use_cases.description: Check whether reported returns include bias controls, drawdown analysis, and out-of-sample evidence.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Basic Backtest
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to outline a backtest for my trading idea. Include required data, assumptions, costs, and validation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Find Bias Risks
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my proposed strategy backtest for look-ahead bias, survivorship bias, overfitting, and unrealistic transaction assumptions.

prompt_templates.title: Choose an Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare event-driven and vectorized backtesting approaches for my strategy. Recommend one design and explain the tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Robust Evaluation Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a walk-forward and Monte Carlo evaluation workflow for my strategy. Include metrics, parameter search rules, and failure criteria.

output_examples.input: A user asks how to test a daily momentum strategy.
output_examples.output: A structured test plan with data requirements, signal timing rules, cost assumptions, train-test splits, and key metrics.

output_examples.input: A user shares high backtest returns and asks whether they are reliable.
output_examples.output: A review checklist covering leakage, survivorship, parameter overfit, transaction costs, capacity, drawdowns, and out-of-sample evidence.

output_examples.input: A user wants to compare several parameter sets.
output_examples.output: A walk-forward workflow that separates optimization windows from test windows and records parameter stability across periods.

best_practices: Use point-in-time data and shift signals so orders cannot use future prices.
Include commissions, slippage, liquidity limits, and market impact assumptions.
Reserve out-of-sample periods and report drawdowns with risk-adjusted returns.
anti_patterns: Optimizing parameters on the full history before reporting final results.
Ignoring delisted securities or other survivorship effects.
Treating example backtest code as production trading infrastructure without validation.

faq.question: Can this skill run a backtest by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and example patterns that an assistant can adapt into code or analysis.

faq.question: Does it include live trading support?
faq.answer: No. The skill focuses on research backtests and strategy validation, not broker execution.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: It is marked as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What data do I need?
faq.answer: You need historical market data with timestamps, prices, and any fields required by the strategy.

faq.question: How does it reduce look-ahead bias?
faq.answer: It emphasizes shifted signals, point-in-time data, and strict separation between training and test periods.

faq.question: Is this financial advice?
faq.answer: No. It is technical guidance for evaluating strategy tests and does not recommend trades.
